attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for confirmation of pregnancy. Her last menstrual period was six weeks ago and she has a positive home pregnancy test. She has a history of Hashimoto thyroiditis diagnosed two years ago but stopped taking levothyroxine six months ago as she was feeling well. She has no other medical conditions and takes no other medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Thyroid function tests reveal: TSH 4.8 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), Free T4 12 pmol/L (9-19).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most significant risk of untreated hypothyroidism in this pregnancy?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with subclinical hypothyroidism in early pregnancy, as evidenced by an elevated TSH with normal Free T4. Thyroid hormones play a crucial role in fetal development, particularly neurological development, as the fetus does not produce its own thyroid hormones until approximately 12 weeks gestation.

Impaired fetal neurodevelopment is the most significant risk of untreated maternal hypothyroidism, particularly during the first trimester. Studies have shown that children born to mothers with untreated hypothyroidism during pregnancy have lower IQ scores and increased risk of cognitive and developmental delays.

Gestational diabetes has a weak association with thyroid dysfunction, but the evidence for causation is not strong, and other risk factors such as BMI and family history are more significant.

Pre-eclampsia has been associated with thyroid dysfunction, but the relationship is not as direct or well-established as the impact on fetal neurodevelopment.

Placental abruption is not specifically associated with hypothyroidism in pregnancy.

Gestational hypertension may be more common in women with hypothyroidism, but this risk is less significant than the impact on fetal neurodevelopment.

Guidelines recommend initiating or adjusting levothyroxine therapy to maintain TSH below 2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester. Women with known thyroid disease require regular monitoring throughout pregnancy.
learningPoints: Untreated hypothyroidism in pregnancy increases risk of miscarriage, preterm birth, and impaired fetal neurodevelopment.
TSH should be maintained below 2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester of pregnancy.
Levothyroxine requirements typically increase by 25-50% during pregnancy due to increased metabolic demands.
references: Cheetham T. Thyroid disease in the perinatal period. Aust Fam Physician [Internet]. 2012 [cited 2024 Nov 22];41(8):578-81. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2012/august/thyroid-disease-in-the-perinatal-period
